title: Lamb madras
subtitle: Hot, sour lamb curry with South Indian spicing
excerpt: Lamb shoulder pieces simmered in a deeply reduced, tomato-based sauce spiced with a heavy hand of chilli, cumin, coriander, and tamarind. A South Indian-influenced curry that sits firmly at the hot end of the heat scale. The sauce is thick, dark, and clinging, with an assertive sourness from tamarind.
sourceNotes: Madras curry is a British-Indian restaurant category named after Madras (Chennai), though the curry itself is a British-Indian adaptation rather than a direct regional dish from Tamil Nadu. It has been a standard heat category on British Indian menus since the 1970s. Traditionally applied to chicken or lamb.

recipe.batchNotes: Better the next day. Doubles well.

content.text: Madras curry is defined by three things: heat from chilli, sourness from tamarind, and a thick, deeply reduced sauce. The chilli level should be properly hot without becoming overwhelming — this is not the hottest curry on the menu (vindaloo claims that), but it should make itself known clearly. The tamarind is the element that distinguishes it from other hot curries: that sour note cuts through the richness and makes the heat more complex.

The sauce needs to reduce until it clings to the meat. After adding the tomatoes, cook them down until there is almost no liquid before adding water — this concentrates the base and gives the finished curry its depth of colour and flavour. A thin madras sauce means the base was not reduced sufficiently.
Brown the lamb and reduce the base

Heat the {{vegetable-oil}} in a large heavy pan over high heat. Brown the {{lamb-shoulder}} in batches, 3 minutes per batch, without crowding. Remove and set aside. Reduce heat to medium. Add the {{onion}} and cook for 12 to 15 minutes until deep golden. Add the {{garlic}}, {{ginger-root}}, {{turmeric}}, {{cumin-ground}}, {{coriander-ground}}, and {{chilli-powder}} and stir for 1 minute. Add the {{tinned-tomatoes}} and cook for 10 to 12 minutes, stirring regularly, until reduced to a thick, paste-like consistency.

Return the lamb to the pan. Add 300 ml water and the {{tamarind-paste}}. Bring to the boil, then cover and reduce to a low simmer. Cook for 60 to 70 minutes, stirring every 15 minutes, until the lamb is tender. Remove the lid for the final 10 minutes to reduce the sauce. Stir in the {{garam-masala}} and season with salt. The sauce should be thick and dark, coating the meat.

items.cause: Base not reduced enough before adding water
items.fix: Remove the lid and simmer on medium heat, stirring, until reduced to the correct consistency
Add chilli powder with the garam masala at the end in small increments. Taste as you go
Add a small pinch of sugar to balance. The sourness should be background, not the lead flavour
Lamb shoulder needs a full simmer — add another splash of water if running dry and continue until it yields easily to a fork

content.text: Madras is the second-hottest standard entry on the British curry-house heat ladder, sitting between bhuna and vindaloo. In British curry-house ordering culture it signals a heat preference rather than a specific origin story — regulars who want heat without the capsaicin challenge of vindaloo order madras. The tamarind sourness distinguishes it from a simple hot curry and rewards eating slowly.
